Solution:

\text{Let } \alpha = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{2u}\right).

\text{Using the reference triangle, we define the sides based on the inverse function:}

x = 2 \, \sqrt{u^{2} - 1}, \quad y = 2, \quad r = 2u

\text{From this, we write the required trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{2}{2u}

\cos(\alpha) = \frac{2 \, \sqrt{u^{2} - 1}}{2u}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\cos(2\alpha) = \cos(2\alpha) = 1 - 2\sin^2(\alpha)

\cos(2\alpha) = 1 - 2\left(\frac{2}{2u}\right)^2

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\cos(2\alpha) = \frac{u^{2} - 2}{u^{2}}

Solution:

\text{Let } \alpha = \cot^{-1}\left(\frac{2u}{5}\right).

\text{Using the reference triangle, we define the sides based on the inverse function:}

x = 2u, \quad y = 5, \quad r = \sqrt{4 \, u^{2} + 25}

\text{From this, we write the required trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{5}{\sqrt{4 \, u^{2} + 25}}

\cos(\alpha) = \frac{2u}{\sqrt{4 \, u^{2} + 25}}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha)

\sin(2\alpha) = 2\left(\frac{5}{\sqrt{4 \, u^{2} + 25}}\right)\left(\frac{2u}{\sqrt{4 \, u^{2} + 25}}\right)

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \frac{20 \, u}{4 \, u^{2} + 25}

Solution:

\text{Let } \alpha = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{3u}{7}\right).

\text{Using the reference triangle, we define the sides based on the inverse function:}

x = \sqrt{-9 \, u^{2} + 49}, \quad y = 3u, \quad r = 7

\text{From this, we write the required trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{3u}{7}

\cos(\alpha) = \frac{\sqrt{-9 \, u^{2} + 49}}{7}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\cos(2\alpha) = \cos(2\alpha) = 1 - 2\sin^2(\alpha)

\cos(2\alpha) = 1 - 2\left(\frac{3u}{7}\right)^2

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\cos(2\alpha) = -\frac{18 \, u^{2} + 49}{49}

Solution:

\text{Let } \alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(u\right).

\text{Using the reference triangle, we define the sides based on the inverse function:}

x = 1, \quad y = u, \quad r = \sqrt{u^{2} + 1}

\text{From this, we write the required trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{u}{\sqrt{u^{2} + 1}}

\cos(\alpha) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{u^{2} + 1}}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\cos(2\alpha) = \cos(2\alpha) = 1 - 2\sin^2(\alpha)

\cos(2\alpha) = 1 - 2\left(\frac{u}{\sqrt{u^{2} + 1}}\right)^2

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\cos(2\alpha) = -\frac{u^{2} + 1}{u^{2} + 1}

Solution:

\text{Let } \alpha = \sec^{-1}\left(u\right).

\text{Using the reference triangle, we define the sides based on the inverse function:}

x = 1, \quad y = \sqrt{u^{2} - 1}, \quad r = u

\text{From this, we write the required trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{\sqrt{u^{2} - 1}}{u}

\cos(\alpha) = \frac{1}{u}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha)

\sin(2\alpha) = 2\left(\frac{\sqrt{u^{2} - 1}}{u}\right)\left(\frac{1}{u}\right)

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \frac{2 \, \sqrt{u^{2} - 1}}{u^{2}}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin(\alpha)\cos(\alpha)

\sin(2\alpha) = 2\left(2u\right)\left(\sqrt{-4 \, u^{2} + 1}\right)

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\sin(2\alpha) = 4 \, \sqrt{-4 \, u^{2} + 1} u

\text{From this, we write the required trigonometric ratios:}

\sin(\alpha) = \frac{3}{2u}

\cos(\alpha) = \frac{\sqrt{4 \, u^{2} - 9}}{2u}

\text{Apply the double-angle identity:}

\cos(2\alpha) = \cos(2\alpha) = 1 - 2\sin^2(\alpha)

\cos(2\alpha) = 1 - 2\left(\frac{3}{2u}\right)^2

\text{Simplify the algebraic expression:}

\cos(2\alpha) = \frac{2 \, u^{2} - 9}{2 \, u^{2}}
